message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.

H-016: Confirm-step double-dip leak check (assert_confirm_only) enforced by confirm_unit_level but not by the discovery/pipeline runners
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:120-165
Description: DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only (firewalls.py:126-145) is the runtime guard that the confirm step touched ONLY held-out ids. confirm_unit_level calls it on the caller-supplied evaluated_ids (confirm.py:188). The two composing runners that also confirm — discover_and_confirm (discovery.py) and the pipeline's DISCOVERY/CONFIRM block — never call assert_confirm_only; they only verify the held-out partition is non-empty and subset the corpus, then trust the study-supplied confirmer to evaluate within it without checking which ids it actually scored. A confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units is caught by the standalone primitive but NOT by either runner. Firewall-B's leak check exists on one parallel mechanism and is absent on the other two.
PoC: Build a confirm_held_out that ignores its held_out arg and evaluates discovery-partition units. confirm_unit_level(hyp, split=split, evaluated_ids=<discovery ids>, ...) raises FirewallViolation 'double-dipping'. The same leak through discover_and_confirm(... confirm=that_callable ...) completes cleanly because the runner never asks the confirmer which ids it evaluated.
Suggested fix: Have the discovery runners obtain (or require) the set of ids the confirmer evaluated and pass it through split.assert_confirm_only before accepting the verdict (e.g. have HeldOutConfirmer return evaluated ids alongside the verdict). At minimum document in both runners that the confirm-time double-dip check is delegated to the study, making the asymmetry with confirm_unit_level explicit.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	"""Discovery runner — Firewall B (discover/confirm separation) enforced by construction.
2	
3	The mirror of :mod:`assay_engine.methodology.adjudication`. In discovery mode the data surfaces
4	candidate hypotheses; Firewall B (METHODOLOGY.md §2, ADR-0005) requires that the data used to
5	*discover* a hypothesis is not the data used to *confirm* it — otherwise discovering a pattern
6	and "testing" it on the same data proves nothing.
7	
8	The engine had the split primitive (:class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`) and a confirm step that
9	refuses discovery ids, but no composition — so the separation held only if a study wired it by
10	hand (same risk Firewall A had before ADR-0008). :func:`discover_and_confirm` makes it
11	structural: it hands the ``discover`` step a corpus containing *only* the discovery partition
12	and the ``confirm`` step a corpus containing *only* the held-out partition, so neither step can
13	accidentally use the other's data.
14	
15	Honest scope (lesson from the adjudication review, ADR-0008): the engine partitions the
16	**structural** data — units, and relations whose *both* endpoints are in the partition. It
17	does NOT carry the corpus's free-form ``metadata`` into a subset (an arbitrary mapping cannot
18	be partitioned safely — a per-unit index in metadata would otherwise leak the other partition
19	wholesale), and it cannot police free-text inside ``Unit``/``Relation.attributes`` that an
20	adapter might use to reference units outside the partition. So: the structural separation is
21	enforced; keeping per-unit data out of metadata/attribute *payloads* is the adapter's
22	obligation (the Firewall-B analogue of the corpus-content caveat in ADR-0005). And, as with
23	adjudication, this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping,
24	not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could).
25	"""
26	
27	from __future__ import annotations
28	
29	import datetime as _dt
30	from typing import Callable, Iterable, Protocol
31	
32	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
33	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
34	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
35	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
36	    TimestampAuthority,
37	    require_preregistered,
38	)
39	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
40	
41	
42	class HeldOutConfirmer(Protocol):
43	    """Study-supplied: confirm one locked, data-surfaced hypothesis on the held-out corpus."""
44	
45	    def __call__(self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, held_out: Corpus) -> Verdict: ...
46	
47	
48	def subset_corpus(corpus: Corpus, ids: Iterable[str]) -> Corpus:
49	    """Return the sub-corpus of ``corpus`` restricted to ``ids``.
50	
51	    Units not in ``ids`` are dropped; a relation is kept only if *both* endpoints are in
52	    ``ids`` (a relation to a unit outside the partition would leak that unit's existence/links
53	    into the partition).
54	
55	    Corpus-level ``metadata`` is deliberately **dropped**, not carried through: it is an
56	    arbitrary mapping the engine cannot partition safely. A per-unit index living in metadata
57	    (e.g. ``{"by_unit": {unit_id: ...}}``) would otherwise hand the discovery step the held-out
58	    partition wholesale — silently defeating Firewall B. Adapters that need partition-scoped
59	    side data must put it on the ``Unit``/``Relation`` records (which *are* partitioned), not in
60	    corpus metadata; free-text inside ``attributes`` that references foreign units remains the
61	    adapter's obligation (ADR-0005 / ADR-0008 honest-scope caveat).
62	    """
63	    keep = frozenset(ids)
64	    units = tuple(u for u in corpus.units if u.unit_id in keep)
65	    relations = tuple(r for r in corpus.relations if r.source_id in keep and r.target_id in keep)
66	    return Corpus(units=units, relations=relations)
67	
68	
69	def discover_and_confirm(
70	    corpus: Corpus,
71	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
72	    *,
73	    discover: Callable[[Corpus], Iterable[Hypothesis]],
74	    confirm: HeldOutConfirmer,
75	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
76	) -> list[Verdict]:
77	    """Run discovery then confirmation with the data separation enforced by construction.
78	
79	    1. ``discover`` is handed a corpus containing only the discovery partition and returns
80	       candidate hypotheses. They must be ``DISCOVERY``-origin and genuinely **pre-registered**
81	       before confirmation — ``require_preregistered`` checks (via ``authority``) that the proof
82	       binds each hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes confirmation.
83	       Discovering and confirming on the same data, or confirming a hypothesis whose content was
84	       changed after locking, is the double-dipping Firewall B exists to prevent.
85	    2. ``confirm`` is handed a corpus containing only the held-out partition and tests each
86	       hypothesis there; the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers.
87	
88	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
89	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
90	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default. A study's ``discover`` step locks each
91	    discovered hypothesis (e.g. via :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.
92	    lock_hypothesis`) before returning it.
93	
94	    Returns the verdicts. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl. ``PreRegistrationError``) on an
95	    empty discovery or held-out partition (split ids that select no corpus units — a vacuous
96	    test), a non-``DISCOVERY`` hypothesis, a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered
97	    before confirmation, or a verdict↔hypothesis misattribution.
98	    """
99	    discovery_corpus = subset_corpus(corpus, split.discovery_ids)
100	    if not discovery_corpus.units:
101	        # the discovery partition selects no actual corpus units — a hypothesis "discovered"
102	        # from zero data is not data-surfaced at all; it would confirm cleanly on the held-out
103	        # set and silently launder a hand-authored claim through Firewall B. Fail loud.
104	        raise FirewallViolation(
105	            "discovery partition selects no corpus units — nothing to discover from "
106	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
107	        )
108	    hypotheses = list(discover(discovery_corpus))  # sees only the discovery partition
109	    if not hypotheses:
110	        # discovery surfaced nothing — a discovery run with zero hypotheses produces no
111	        # confirmatory verdict at all and is indistinguishable from a silently broken
112	        # ``discover`` callable that forgot to return. Fail loud rather than complete vacuously
113	        # (pass 3, #F-042; mirrors the empty-partition guards above).
114	        raise FirewallViolation(
115	            "discover() returned no hypotheses — a discovery run with zero hypotheses is "
116	            "vacuous and cannot produce a confirmatory verdict"
117	        )
118	
119	    held_out = subset_corpus(corpus, split.confirm_ids)  # disjoint from discovery (split invariant)
120	    if not held_out.units:
121	        # the confirmation partition selects no actual corpus units — confirming on an empty
122	        # held-out set is a vacuous test (the split ids don't match the corpus). Fail loud.
123	        raise FirewallViolation(
124	            "confirmation partition selects no corpus units — nothing to confirm on "
125	            "(do split ids match the corpus unit ids?)"
126	        )
127	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
128	    # within-run hypothesis uniqueness (pass 3, #F-018): a ``discover`` step returning the same
129	    # hypothesis_id twice would append two verdicts for one hypothesis, inflating the verdict
130	    # count. The pipeline runner guards this via ``_require_unique_ids``; mirror it in the
131	    # public standalone runner so both entry points enforce the same identity invariant.
132	    seen_hypothesis_ids: set[str] = set()
133	    for hypothesis in hypotheses:
134	        if hypothesis.hypothesis_id in seen_hypothesis_ids:
135	            raise FirewallViolation(
136	                f"duplicate hypothesis_id {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} returned by discover() — "
137	                "each discovered hypothesis must have a unique id"
138	            )
139	        seen_hypothesis_ids.add(hypothesis.hypothesis_id)
140	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY:
141	            raise FirewallViolation(
142	                f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is {hypothesis.origin.value!r}; the "
143	                "discovery runner expects DISCOVERY-origin (data-surfaced) hypotheses"
144	            )
145	        # Pre-registration enforced by the runner: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content
146	        # (no post-lock content swap) and its attested lock time must precede this confirmation.
147	        require_preregistered(
148	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
149	        )
150	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out)
151	        # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing verdict.hypothesis_id would
152	        # raise an opaque AttributeError instead of a typed firewall error (pass 4, #G-011 —
153	        # mirrors the pipeline/adjudication guards from #F-021).
154	        if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):
155	            raise FirewallViolation(
156	                f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis "
157	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"
158	            )
159	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
160	            raise FirewallViolation(
161	                f"verdict reports hypothesis_id {verdict.hypothesis_id!r} for hypothesis "
162	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
163	            )
164	        verdicts.append(verdict)
165	    return verdicts
166

1	"""The two firewalls, enforced as runtime primitives (ADR-0005, METHODOLOGY.md §2).
2	
3	Both separations are enforced in code and exercised by tests — never asserted only in prose.
4	
5	Firewall A — claim-blindness:
6	    The baseline must be built blind to external claims. The *structural* guarantee is that
7	    the baseline builder is never handed an ``ExternalClaimsSource`` (its ``build`` signature
8	    takes only a ``Corpus``) and that ``StudyDefinition`` refuses a claims source outside
9	    adjudication mode. On top of that, :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` gives the claims source
10	    *custody*: hand the source to the guard, and the only way to obtain it is
11	    ``release()``/``access()``, which raise while the guard is sealed. Baseline construction
12	    runs inside ``sealed()``; adjudication runs after it.
13	
14	    Caveat (audit pass 1, issue #1): the guard cannot police a baseline builder that ignores
15	    it and reads external judgments smuggled into ``Corpus`` metadata/relations — that path
16	    is closed by the adapter contract (see schema.py), not by this guard. The guard is
17	    custodial defense-in-depth over the type-level separation, not the sole barrier.
18	
19	Firewall B — discover/confirm separation:
20	    The data used to discover a hypothesis must not be the data used to confirm it.
21	    :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit` holds disjoint, non-empty discovery and confirmation id
22	    sets and refuses to confirm on any id the discovery phase touched (or on an empty set).
23	"""
24	
25	from __future__ import annotations
26	
27	from contextlib import contextmanager
28	from dataclasses import dataclass
29	from typing import Generic, Iterable, Iterator, TypeVar
30	
31	C = TypeVar("C")
32	
33	
34	class FirewallViolation(RuntimeError):
35	    """Raised when a methodological firewall is breached at runtime."""
36	
37	
38	class ClaimBlindGuard(Generic[C]):
39	    """Firewall A. While sealed, the held claims source is unreachable.
40	
41	    Custodial usage (preferred) — the guard owns the source, so the baseline pipeline holds
42	    the guard rather than the source and cannot read claims while sealed::
43	
44	        guard = ClaimBlindGuard(claims_source)
45	        with guard.sealed():
46	            corpus = parser.parse(source)            # baseline build — claims unreachable
47	            baseline = build_baseline(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
48	        claims = guard.release()                      # adjudication — guard released
49	
50	    ``access(obj)`` remains for defensive checks at call sites that hold a reference directly:
51	    it returns ``obj`` when unsealed and raises while sealed.
52	    """
53	
54	    def __init__(self, claims_source: C | None = None) -> None:
55	        self._sealed = False
56	        self._claims_source = claims_source
57	
58	    @property
59	    def is_sealed(self) -> bool:
60	        return self._sealed
61	
62	    @contextmanager
63	    def sealed(self) -> Iterator["ClaimBlindGuard[C]"]:
64	        prior = self._sealed
65	        self._sealed = True
66	        try:
67	            yield self
68	        finally:
69	            self._sealed = prior
70	
71	    def release(self) -> C:
72	        """Return the custodial claims source, or raise while sealed / if none was given."""
73	        if self._sealed:
74	            raise FirewallViolation(
75	                "Firewall A: external claims released during blind baseline construction"
76	            )
77	        if self._claims_source is None:
78	            raise FirewallViolation("Firewall A: no claims source is under this guard's custody")
79	        return self._claims_source
80	
81	    def access(self, claims_source: C) -> C:
82	        """Return ``claims_source`` only if the guard is not sealed; else raise."""
83	        if self._sealed:
84	            raise FirewallViolation(
85	                "Firewall A: external claims accessed during blind baseline construction"
86	            )
87	        return claims_source
88	
89	
90	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
91	class DiscoverConfirmSplit:
92	    """Firewall B. Disjoint discovery / confirmation id partitions for unit-level claims."""
93	
94	    discovery_ids: frozenset[str]
95	    confirm_ids: frozenset[str]
96	
97	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
98	        # Coerce to frozenset so the raw constructor (public API) cannot yield a "frozen" record
99	        # backed by mutable sets — otherwise the disjointness invariant below could be defeated by
100	        # post-construction in-place mutation (s.confirm_ids.add(...)) (#135).
101	        if not isinstance(self.discovery_ids, frozenset):
102	            object.__setattr__(self, "discovery_ids", frozenset(self.discovery_ids))
103	        if not isinstance(self.confirm_ids, frozenset):
104	            object.__setattr__(self, "confirm_ids", frozenset(self.confirm_ids))
105	        overlap = self.discovery_ids & self.confirm_ids
106	        if overlap:
107	            raise FirewallViolation(
108	                f"Firewall B: discovery and confirmation partitions overlap on "
109	                f"{len(overlap)} id(s), e.g. {sorted(overlap)[:3]}"
110	            )
111	        # An empty confirmation partition means there is nothing held out to confirm on; a
112	        # unit-level held-out split with no held-out object is invalid by construction. The
113	        # genuine no-holdout case must use the whole-corpus null path (audit pass 1, issue #5).
114	        if not self.confirm_ids:
115	            raise FirewallViolation(
116	                "Firewall B: confirmation partition is empty — a unit-level held-out split "
117	                "must hold out at least one object (use whole-corpus confirmation otherwise)"
118	            )
119	
120	    @classmethod
121	    def from_partition(
122	        cls, discovery_ids: Iterable[str], confirm_ids: Iterable[str]
123	    ) -> "DiscoverConfirmSplit":
124	        return cls(frozenset(discovery_ids), frozenset(confirm_ids))
125	
126	    def assert_confirm_only(self, ids: Iterable[str]) -> None:
127	        """Raise if ``ids`` is empty or any of it was seen during discovery (double-dip)."""
128	        ids = frozenset(ids)
129	        if not ids:
130	            raise FirewallViolation(
131	                "Firewall B: confirmatory test evaluated zero held-out objects — a verdict "
132	                "cannot be produced from an empty confirmation set (audit pass 1, issue #5)"
133	            )
134	        leaked = ids & self.discovery_ids
135	        if leaked:
136	            raise FirewallViolation(
137	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test touched {len(leaked)} discovery id(s) — "
138	                f"double-dipping, e.g. {sorted(leaked)[:3]}"
139	            )
140	        unknown = ids - self.confirm_ids
141	        if unknown:
142	            raise FirewallViolation(
143	                f"Firewall B: confirmatory test used {len(unknown)} id(s) outside the "
144	                f"confirmation partition, e.g. {sorted(unknown)[:3]}"
145	            )
146

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, validate the supplied
14	statistics, and map an outcome onto one of the three verdicts honestly (including
15	``indeterminate``).
16	
17	Direction handling (audit pass 1, issue #2): a claim predicts the statistic lies in a
18	particular tail of the null. The empirical p-value is computed against *that* tail, so a
19	claim predicting a low value is confirmable, and a genuine contradiction (significant in the
20	opposite tail) is detectable — both were impossible under the previous direction-blind
21	upper-tail-only implementation.
22	"""
23	
24	from __future__ import annotations
25	
26	import datetime as _dt
27	import math
28	import warnings
29	from typing import Iterable, Sequence
30	
31	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
32	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Direction, Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
33	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
34	    TimestampAuthority,
35	    require_preregistered,
36	)
37	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
38	
39	
40	def _gate_preregistration(hypothesis: Hypothesis, authority: TimestampAuthority | None) -> None:
41	    """Pre-registration gate for the confirm primitives.
42	
43	    If ``authority`` is supplied, run the full :func:`require_preregistered` check (content
44	    binding + verifiable timestamp + lock-before-this-moment) — so a study calling a confirm
45	    primitive *directly* (outside a runner) gets the same guarantee the runners enforce. If it
46	    is ``None``, fall back to the cheap presence gate (:func:`require_locked`); the caller has
47	    then opted out of real verification and owns that choice.
48	    """
49	    if authority is not None:
50	        require_preregistered(
51	            hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=_dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
52	        )
53	    else:
54	        # Falling back to the cheap presence gate means a hand-set timestamp_proof string passes
55	        # WITHOUT any cryptographic verification. Warn so a direct caller cannot silently skip real
56	        # pre-registration (#G-006); the runners always supply an authority and never hit this.
57	        warnings.warn(
58	            "confirm primitive called with authority=None: pre-registration is verified by a "
59	            "PRESENCE check only (lock fields populated), NOT cryptographically. Pass a "
60	            "TimestampAuthority to verify the proof binds the hypothesis and precedes confirmation.",
61	            stacklevel=3,
62	        )
63	        require_locked(hypothesis)
64	
65	
66	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
67	    """Cheap **presence** gate: refuse a hypothesis that carries no lock fields at all.
68	
69	    This is defense-in-depth, not the methodology-grade check. It does not verify the proof
70	    binds the content or that the lock precedes confirmation — that is
71	    :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.require_preregistered`, which the
72	    confirmatory *runners* (:mod:`adjudication`, :mod:`discovery`) call before they confirm.
73	    A study driving :func:`confirm_whole_corpus`/:func:`confirm_unit_level` directly (outside a
74	    runner) should call ``require_preregistered`` itself to get the real guarantee.
75	    """
76	    if not hypothesis.locked:
77	        raise FirewallViolation(
78	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
79	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
80	        )
81	
82	
83	def _validate_alpha(alpha: float) -> None:
84	    # A significance threshold of 0.5 or more is not a meaningful one-sided level, and
85	    # admitting it lets both tails register "significant" at once (alpha < 0.5 guarantees
86	    # p_support + p_contra >= 1 cannot both fall below alpha) — fix-review nit on issue #2.
87	    if not (0.0 < alpha < 0.5):
88	        raise ValueError(f"alpha must be in (0, 0.5); got {alpha}")
89	
90	
91	def _validate_pvalue(p_value: float) -> None:
92	    if not (0.0 <= p_value <= 1.0):
93	        raise ValueError(f"p_value must be in [0, 1]; got {p_value}")
94	
95	
96	def _validate_finite(name: str, value: float) -> None:
97	    if not math.isfinite(value):
98	        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be a finite number; got {value}")
99	
100	
101	def verdict_from_pvalue(
102	    hypothesis_id: str,
103	    *,
104	    statistic: float,
105	    p_value: float,
106	    alpha: float,
107	    powered: bool,
108	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
109	    test_description: str = "confirmatory test",
110	) -> Verdict:
111	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
112	
113	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
114	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
115	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
116	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here).
117	
118	    ``p_value`` and ``direction_supports_claim`` must be derived by the caller from a test
119	    appropriate to the claim's predicted direction. For a unit-level held-out test the engine
120	    is handed only an opaque ``statistic`` + ``p_value`` and cannot itself infer whether the
121	    statistic supports or contradicts the claim — so ``direction_supports_claim`` is a trusted
122	    caller obligation, ``statistic`` is recorded for provenance only (it does NOT gate the
123	    verdict), and the deciding flag is recorded in ``evidence`` so the verdict is re-derivable
124	    (audit pass 2, issue #20). Inputs are validated (issue #4) and the recorded decision rule
125	    reflects the caller's ``test_description``, not a hard-coded sidedness label (issue #2).
126	    """
127	    _validate_pvalue(p_value)
128	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
129	    _validate_finite("statistic", statistic)
130	
131	    rule = f"{test_description} at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
132	    common = dict(
133	        statistic=statistic,
134	        threshold=alpha,
135	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "direction_supports_claim": direction_supports_claim},
136	    )
137	    if not powered:
138	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
139	    if p_value <= alpha:
140	        if direction_supports_claim:
141	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
142	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
143	    return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common)
144	
145	
146	def confirm_unit_level(
147	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
148	    *,
149	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
150	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
151	    statistic: float,
152	    p_value: float,
153	    alpha: float,
154	    powered: bool,
155	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
156	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
157	) -> Verdict:
158	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only.
159	
160	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
161	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
162	
163	    Direction (pass 4, #G-017): unlike :func:`confirm_whole_corpus` — where the verdict polarity
164	    is derived from the pre-registered ``predicted_direction`` tail — the unit-level verdict is
165	    governed SOLELY by the caller-supplied ``direction_supports_claim`` flag (the engine sees only
166	    an opaque held-out ``statistic``/``p_value`` and cannot itself infer the tail). The
167	    hypothesis's ``predicted_direction`` is therefore advisory at unit level: it is shape-validated
168	    below for safety but does NOT decide the verdict. A study that wants the locked tail to bind the
169	    unit-level decision must derive ``direction_supports_claim`` from it in its own confirmer.
170	    """
171	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
172	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
173	    # Defense-in-depth direction validation (pass 3, #F-002): the unit-level path takes the
174	    # deciding direction as a trusted caller flag (``direction_supports_claim``) and so never
175	    # routed through ``_resolve_direction`` — which left a locked-but-invalid
176	    # ``predicted_direction`` (e.g. "UP") entirely unchecked here. ``Hypothesis.__post_init__``
177	    # now rejects such values at construction, but re-validate any pre-registered direction here
178	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
179	    # against an unrecognized tail.
180	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
181	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
182	    ):
183	        raise ValueError(
184	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
185	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
186	        )
187	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
188	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
189	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
190	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
191	        statistic=statistic,
192	        p_value=p_value,
193	        alpha=alpha,
194	        powered=powered,
195	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
196	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
197	    )
198	
199	
200	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
201	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
202	    n = len(null)
203	    if tail == "greater":
204	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
205	    else:
206	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
207	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
208	
209	
210	def _resample_stability(
211	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
212	) -> float:
213	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
214	
215	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
216	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
217	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
218	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
219	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
220	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
221	
222	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
223	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
224	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
225	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
226	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
227	    """
228	    n = len(null)
229	    try:
230	        import numpy as np
231	
232	        # Use searchsorted on a sorted null (O((R+N) log N) time, O(R) memory) rather than the
233	        # R×N broadcast boolean matrix (#G-010): the matrix peaks at ~R*N bytes (250MB for
234	        # R=5000,N=50000) and can OOM where the pure-Python loop would not. The integer
235	        # extreme-counts are IDENTICAL to _empirical_p: for 'greater', count(null >= r) = N -
236	        # searchsorted(sorted, r, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= r) = searchsorted(sorted, r,
237	        # 'right'). Then (extreme+1)/(n+1) <= alpha, same as the pure path.
238	        sorted_null = np.sort(np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64))
239	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
240	        if tail == "greater":
241	            extreme = n - np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="left")
242	        else:
243	            extreme = np.searchsorted(sorted_null, res_arr, side="right")
244	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
245	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
246	    except ImportError:
247	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
248	        return significant / len(resamples)
249	
250	
251	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
252	
253	
254	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
255	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time argument is
256	    optional and, if given, must MATCH the pre-registered one — it can cross-check, never supply.
257	
258	    A hypothesis reaching here has already passed the confirm gate, so it is locked and its
259	    ``predicted_direction`` is part of the cryptographic binding. A ``None`` locked direction
260	    therefore means the tail was *not* pre-registered; accepting a confirm-time direction in that
261	    case would let the analyst pick the tail AFTER seeing the data (HARKing), flipping
262	    supported↔contradicted on a hypothesis whose lock attests "no direction" (audit pass 4,
263	    #G-001). The tail must be committed before confirmation, so a directionless locked hypothesis
264	    is refused outright rather than completed with a post-hoc direction.
265	
266	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}``; any other value is
267	    rejected (an unrecognized tail would silently fall through to 'less' in :func:`_empirical_p`,
268	    flipping the verdict — audit pass 2, #128).
269	    """
270	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
271	    if locked_dir is None:
272	        raise ValueError(
273	            "predicted_direction was not pre-registered: a whole-corpus confirmatory hypothesis "
274	            "must lock its tail ('greater' or 'less') BEFORE confirmation. Supplying a confirm-time "
275	            "direction for a directionless locked hypothesis selects the tail after seeing the data "
276	            "(HARKing) and is refused (#G-001)."
277	        )
278	    if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
279	        raise ValueError(
280	            "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
281	            f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
282	        )
283	    resolved = locked_dir
284	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
285	        raise ValueError(
286	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
287	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
288	        )
289	    return resolved
290	
291	
292	def confirm_whole_corpus(
293	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
294	    *,
295	    observed: float,
296	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
297	    alpha: float,
298	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
299	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
300	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,
301	    authority: TimestampAuthority | None = None,
302	) -> Verdict:
303	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + measured stability.
304	
305	    Pass ``authority`` to verify pre-registration in full (content binding + timestamp +
306	    lock-before-now); omit it to fall back to the presence gate (see :func:`require_locked`).
307	
308	    The predicted tail is taken from the hypothesis's pre-registered ``predicted_direction``
309	    (issue #24). The pattern must (a) beat the null at ``alpha`` in the predicted tail and
310	    (b) be **stable across resamples** — measured by the engine from ``resample_statistics``,
311	    not asserted by the caller (audit pass 2, issue #21). Outcomes:
312	
313	    - significant in the predicted tail AND stable → ``supported``;
314	    - significant in the *opposite* tail → ``contradicted``;
315	    - unstable, not significant, or stability not assessed → ``indeterminate``.
316	
317	    If ``resample_statistics`` is omitted, stability cannot be measured, so ``supported`` is
318	    unavailable and the verdict is ``indeterminate`` ("stability not assessed").
319	    """
320	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
321	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
322	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
323	    _validate_alpha(alpha)
324	    _validate_finite("observed", observed)
325	    if not null_distribution:
326	        raise ValueError(
327	            "a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation"
328	        )
329	    for v in null_distribution:
330	        _validate_finite("null_distribution value", v)
331	    if not (0.0 < stability_threshold <= 1.0):
332	        raise ValueError(f"stability_threshold must be in (0, 1]; got {stability_threshold}")
333	
334	    direction = _resolve_direction(hypothesis, predicted_direction)
335	    opposite: Direction = "less" if direction == "greater" else "greater"
336	    p_support = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, direction)
337	    p_contra = _empirical_p(null_distribution, observed, opposite)
338	    n = len(null_distribution)
339	
340	    stability: float | None = None
341	    contra_stability: float | None = None
342	    if resample_statistics is not None:
343	        if not resample_statistics:
344	            raise ValueError("resample_statistics, if given, must be non-empty")
345	        for r in resample_statistics:
346	            _validate_finite("resample statistic", r)
347	        stability = _resample_stability(resample_statistics, null_distribution, direction, alpha)
348	        # Also measure how consistently the resamples reproduce the OPPOSITE-tail significance, so a
349	        # contradiction's robustness is recorded in evidence even though (by design) a contradiction
350	        # is reportable without it (#G-005).
351	        contra_stability = _resample_stability(
352	            resample_statistics, null_distribution, opposite, alpha
353	        )
354	    stable = stability is not None and stability >= stability_threshold
355	
356	    rule = (
357	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}, predicted tail '{direction}'; "
358	        f"stability across resamples >= {stability_threshold} (engine-measured)"
359	    )
360	    evidence = {
361	        "p_support": p_support,
362	        "p_contradict": p_contra,
363	        "null_n": n,
364	        "predicted_direction": direction,
365	        "stability": stability,
366	        "contradiction_stability": contra_stability,
367	        "stability_threshold": stability_threshold,
368	        "n_resamples": len(resample_statistics) if resample_statistics is not None else 0,
369	    }
370	    common = dict(statistic=observed, threshold=alpha, evidence=evidence)
371	
372	    # A contradiction (significant in the OPPOSITE tail) is reportable without a stability gate, and
373	    # this asymmetry with `supported` is INTENTIONAL (#G-005): support is a positive claim that must
374	    # reproduce to be decisive, whereas a statistic landing significantly in the wrong tail is
375	    # evidence OF the opposite — not absence of evidence — so it stands on the observed effect.
376	    # `contradiction_stability` is recorded in evidence (when resamples were supplied) so an auditor
377	    # can see whether the contradiction also reproduces, even though it is not gated on it.
378	    if p_contra <= alpha:
379	        return Verdict.contradicted(
380	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="significant in the opposite tail", **common
381	        )
382	    if stability is None:
383	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
384	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
385	            rule,
386	            notes="stability not assessed (no resamples supplied)",
387	            **common,
388	        )
389	    if not stable:
390	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
391	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
392	        )
393	    if p_support <= alpha:
394	        return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
395	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
396	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
397	    )
398

content.text:
I have confirmed both runner code paths. The PoC describes the asymmetry accurately. Let me verify the PoC mechanics: that `confirm_unit_level` would raise on discovery ids while the runner would not check evaluated ids at all.

The facts:
- `firewalls.py:126-145` — `assert_confirm_only` is the leak check.
- `confirm.py:188` — `confirm_unit_level` calls `split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)`.
- `discovery.py:69-165` — `discover_and_confirm` calls `subset_corpus`, checks partitions non-empty, validates origin/preregistration/verdict-attribution, but **never** obtains or checks which ids the confirmer evaluated. The `HeldOutConfirmer` protocol (line 42-45) returns only a `Verdict`, no evaluated-ids.
- `pipeline.py:558-584` — CONFIRM block likewise: calls `plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out)`, validates Verdict type and hypothesis-id attribution, but never calls `assert_confirm_only`.
- grep confirms `assert_confirm_only` has exactly one caller in src.

The PoC is sound: a confirmer that ignores its `held_out` corpus and internally evaluates discovery-partition units is caught by `confirm_unit_level` (it passes evaluated_ids explicitly), but `discover_and_confirm`/pipeline never pass evaluated_ids — the confirmer receives a `Corpus`, returns a `Verdict`, and nothing checks which ids it scored.

Now the severity assessment. This is an honest-scope caveat already partly documented. The module docstring (discovery.py:23-24) explicitly states: "this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." The structural firewall is that the confirmer is *handed only the held-out subcorpus* (line 119, 562). A confirmer that "internally pulls in discovery-partition units" would have to source them from outside the handed corpus — it does not receive the discovery corpus or the full corpus. So the structural separation does hold for accidental leaks; the gap is for a confirmer that deliberately reaches outside its handed corpus, which the docstring already disclaims as out-of-scope (only process isolation could prevent it).

The genuine finding is the **asymmetry**: `confirm_unit_level` offers a stronger check (it asks for evaluated_ids) that the runners don't replicate. This is a real, accurately-described inconsistency, but it is a defense-in-depth/documentation gap rather than a true production exploitable leak, because the runners enforce separation structurally (the confirmer can't accidentally see discovery data) and the willful-reflection case is already a documented out-of-scope limitation.

This lowers it from a critical/high firewall-bypass to a low-severity hardening item (the suggested fix is itself "at minimum document the asymmetry").

input.reasoning:
The factual claims are accurate against current code. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (firewalls.py:126-145) is the per-id double-dip leak check, and a src-wide grep shows it has exactly ONE caller: confirm_unit_level at confirm.py:188, where evaluated_ids is an explicit parameter. The two composing runners do NOT call it: discover_and_confirm (discovery.py:69-165) subsets the corpus (subset_corpus), guards that both partitions are non-empty (lines 100-126), validates DISCOVERY origin, pre-registration, and verdict<->hypothesis attribution (lines 133-163), but never obtains or checks which ids the confirmer scored — the HeldOutConfirmer protocol (discovery.py:42-45) returns only a Verdict, no evaluated-ids. The pipeline CONFIRM block (pipeline.py:558-584) is identical: it calls plan.confirm_held_out(h, held_out), validates Verdict type (566-570) and hypothesis-id attribution (571-575), but never calls assert_confirm_only. So Firewall-B's per-id leak check exists on confirm_unit_level and is absent on both runners — the asymmetry is real and as described. The PoC reproduces: a confirmer that returns a verdict without honestly scoring only held-out ids is caught by confirm_unit_level (which is handed evaluated_ids) but not by either runner (which never asks the confirmer which ids it scored).

I downgrade severity, however. The runners enforce the separation STRUCTURALLY by handing the confirmer only the held-out sub-corpus (discovery.py:119, pipeline.py:562) — the confirmer is never given the discovery partition or the full corpus, so it cannot ACCIDENTALLY double-dip. The finding's specific exploit ("a confirmer that internally pulls in discovery-partition units") requires the confirmer to willfully reach for data outside its handed corpus, which the discovery.py module docstring (lines 22-24) already explicitly disclaims as out of scope: "this is a *signature-level* guarantee — it prevents accidental double-dipping, not a step that willfully reflects into the caller's frame (only process isolation could)." Thus the genuine, novel issue is the inconsistency between confirm_unit_level offering a stronger evaluated-ids check that the runners don't replicate (and don't even document the omission of) — a defense-in-depth and documentation gap, not an exploitable firewall bypass. The suggested fix's own minimum bar is "document the asymmetry," consistent with a low-severity hardening item.

content.text:
I have completed the verification of finding H-016.

Confirmed: true, severity low.

The asymmetry is real and accurately described. `DiscoverConfirmSplit.assert_confirm_only` (`/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/firewalls.py:126-145`) has exactly one caller in src — `confirm_unit_level` at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:188`. Neither `discover_and_confirm` (`/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/discovery.py:69-165`) nor the pipeline CONFIRM block (`/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:558-584`) calls it; both only validate Verdict type and hypothesis-id attribution. The `HeldOutConfirmer` protocol (`discovery.py:42-45`) returns only a `Verdict`, so the runners have no way to check which ids the confirmer scored.

I downgraded to low because the runners enforce the separation structurally (the confirmer is handed only the held-out sub-corpus, so it cannot accidentally see discovery data), and the willful-reflection exploit is already explicitly documented as out-of-scope in the `discovery.py` module docstring (lines 22-24). The genuine issue is the defense-in-depth/documentation inconsistency, which matches the suggested fix's minimum bar of documenting the asymmetry.
